title: Tree-guided excerpt cut changes rightful atom grouping
scenario_description: The same source passage is cut differently because the current taxonomy leaf expects a certain topic split, so the excerpter silently adjusts the grouping to fit the tree.
failure_chain: current tree shape -> excerpt grouping pressure -> wrong cut -> silent corruption -> downstream placement and study built on wrong unit
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It corrupts excerpt truth at the earliest decisive stage while still looking structured and plausible.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than visible wrong placement because the content itself becomes unsafe rather than merely mislocated.
study_effect: Years of study can be built on a false unit without obvious warning.

title: Changing tree granulation changes excerpt outputs
scenario_description: The same source passage produces different excerpt outputs when run under a correctly granulated tree, an undergranulated tree, and an overgranulated tree.
failure_chain: tree change -> excerpt output change -> taxonomy dependence revealed -> excerpt truth destabilized
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It proves the wrong stage has influenced excerpting and destroys the idea of rightful source-governed output.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than either A or B as pure placement failures because it means the tree is rewriting knowledge rather than organizing it.
study_effect: Any learned excerpt may become suspect after tree revision.

title: Correct excerpt placed at the wrong leaf
scenario_description: The excerpt itself is right, but it appears under the wrong taxonomy leaf.
failure_chain: correct excerpt -> wrong placement -> user sees mismatch -> audit or confusion
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It can still mislead study flow and confidence, especially if repeated.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than excerpt corruption because the user can often notice the mismatch and repair direction is clearer.
study_effect: Study order and local understanding are disturbed, but underlying content truth may survive.

title: Overgranulated tree fragments one natural topic into multiple forced leaves
scenario_description: A natural topic is split across several leaves that each contain only part of what should have remained conceptually together.
failure_chain: overgranulated tree -> forced fragmentation -> user must reconstruct common denominator -> deeper confusion
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It makes the science look more fractured than it is and burdens the user with a harder reconstruction puzzle.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than undergranulation because reassembling cut-up conceptual units is harder than noticing a missing split.
study_effect: The user may memorize fragments without owning the real unity of the topic.

title: Undergranulated tree keeps distinct subtopics merged together
scenario_description: Two or more real subtopics remain under one coarse leaf.
failure_chain: undergranulated tree -> broad leaf -> user notices distinct clusters -> missing split inferred
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It blurs distinctions and lowers precision.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than overgranulation because the repair direction is usually clearer: split deeper rather than reassemble fragments.
study_effect: The user may have to solve an extra granulation step while studying, but the pieces remain visible together.

title: Blind processing produces inconsistent excerpt boundaries across similar passages
scenario_description: Without usable structure or stable criteria, similar source patterns get excerpted differently across passages or books.
failure_chain: blind processing -> unstable decisions -> inconsistent excerpt families -> user confidence erodes
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It creates boundary arbitrariness and makes the system feel unreliable even if corruption is not yet proven.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than silent taxonomy-driven corruption if boundaries remain source-governed, but still a real strength of the B-side argument.
study_effect: The user cannot trust that similar knowledge has been treated consistently.

title: Blind processing misses a real distinction and keeps atoms too coarse
scenario_description: Working with too little guidance, the system fails to recognize a real distinction and produces overly broad excerpts or placements.
failure_chain: low guidance -> missed distinction -> coarse units -> blurred downstream placement
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It shows the genuine risk on the structurelessness side: chaos or missed distinctions can damage usefulness.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Still less dangerous than taxonomy-contaminated corruption if the resulting units remain visibly broad rather than silently false.
study_effect: The user must do extra mental splitting and may lose precision.

title: Premature structure imports school/topic bias into a neutral source passage
scenario_description: A neutral passage is cut or framed as if it belongs to one pre-decided doctrinal box because the taxonomy already expects that relation.
failure_chain: prior box expectation -> biased cut or frame -> excerpt looks cleaner than it should -> downstream misreading
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It is not merely bad placement; it is a hidden reinterpretation of the source through the tree.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than blind inconsistency because it can look highly polished while being wrong in substance.
study_effect: The user learns a pre-framed reading and may never realize the source was more open or differently structured.

title: Placement revision silently rewrites already-frozen excerpts
scenario_description: A later tree redesign retroactively changes excerpt grouping or boundaries in order to fit the new placement.
failure_chain: tree redesign -> excerpt rewrite -> previous study invalidated -> trust collapses
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It destroys the promised independence between excerpt truth and later structural refinement.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than a plain wrong leaf because it reaches backward and rewrites what was supposedly already true.
study_effect: Prior memorization becomes suspect and the user cannot tell what was truly source-derived.

title: Visible wrong placement triggers an audit before deep memorization
scenario_description: A user opens a leaf and immediately sees that the excerpt group belongs elsewhere.
failure_chain: visible mismatch -> suspicion -> audit -> correction before deep transfer
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It still costs confidence and time.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than silent corruption precisely because it is visible and can stop trust transfer early.
study_effect: Study is interrupted, but deeper poisoning may be prevented.

title: Silent excerpt corruption discovered after years of study
scenario_description: After long-term study, the user compares the library against the source and realizes a supposedly rightful excerpt was wrong.
failure_chain: silent corruption -> years of memorization and teaching -> delayed discovery -> global trust poisoning
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It puts not just one leaf but the excerpting engine and knowledge base in question.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than visible misplacement because the user may have built identity, teaching, and recall on the wrong unit.
study_effect: Previously learned material becomes suspect, and future study may freeze from mistrust.

title: Overgranulated placement creates false contradictions between sibling leaves
scenario_description: Because one natural issue was overcut into separate leaves, related excerpts look like contradictory positions instead of parts of one broader unit.
failure_chain: overgranulation -> fragmented reading -> false contradiction impression -> study confusion
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It distorts how the user sees the science even when excerpts themselves are intact.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than undergranulation because fragmentation can produce positively misleading structure rather than merely coarse structure.
study_effect: The user spends energy reconciling an artifact of bad placement instead of learning the science.

title: Undergranulated placement hides a deeper split but keeps the relevant excerpts together
scenario_description: A leaf is too broad, but the relevant excerpts remain visible together so the missing distinction can be noticed.
failure_chain: broad leaf -> visible mixed clusters -> user senses missing granulation
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It still lowers precision and wastes study effort.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than overgranulation because the pieces are not already scattered into a harder reconstruction puzzle.
study_effect: The user sees the need for a deeper split and can often mentally perform it.

title: Common-denominator confusion during overgranulated study
scenario_description: The user has to infer how multiple small leaves belong together after the topic has been cut too finely.
failure_chain: overcut tree -> user reconstructs common denominator -> extra cognitive burden -> confusion
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: The user is forced into synthetic work that the tree should have already solved.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than undergranulated blur because reconstruction from fragments is harder than noticing that one bucket is too broad.
study_effect: Memory and understanding become noisier because the natural conceptual unity is obscured.

title: Search and ranking amplify a wrong box until it feels authoritative
scenario_description: Even when the excerpt itself is intact, ranking and presentation repeatedly surface it under the wrong conceptual box and make the box feel true.
failure_chain: placement/ranking bias -> repeated exposure -> false confidence in wrong structural framing
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: Visible mistakes can still become persuasive when the interface repeatedly endorses them.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than excerpt corruption because the source unit survives, but more dangerous than a one-off visible misplacement because presentation amplifies it.
study_effect: The user may internalize the wrong conceptual placement even if the excerpt text is not corrupt.

title: Placement-fit validation silently rejects rightful excerpts
scenario_description: A later validation stage uses tree fit as a proxy for correctness and silently suppresses or alters excerpts that do not fit the current taxonomy well.
failure_chain: tree-fit validator -> rightful excerpt rejected or reshaped -> silent knowledge loss -> false cleanliness
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It hides a second taxonomy-biased excerpter inside validation while giving the appearance of higher quality control.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than visible wrong placement because it silently changes what counts as a valid excerpt at all.
study_effect: Important but awkward source units disappear or are mutated before the user ever sees them.

title: Same source passage processed guided vs blind yields materially different outputs
scenario_description: One pipeline run uses prior structure, another does not, and the outputs differ materially in boundaries or grouping.
failure_chain: two processing modes -> materially divergent outputs -> uncertainty over rightful excerpt -> trust fracture
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It reveals that boundary truth is unstable under design choices rather than anchored firmly in the source.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: More dangerous than plain blind inconsistency because it exposes direct contamination by guidance choice itself.
study_effect: The user can no longer know which output represents the source rather than the pipeline configuration.

title: Blind no-structure processing creates superficially usable but uneven excerpt families
scenario_description: The outputs are not obviously corrupt, but comparable materials are treated unevenly across the corpus, producing a low-grade trust leak.
failure_chain: blind processing -> uneven decisions -> local plausibility -> global trust erosion
why_this_failure_is_dangerous: It may not trigger immediate collapse, but it creates cumulative doubt about consistency and discipline.
why_it_is_more_or_less_dangerous_than_th: Less dangerous than silent corruption that changes excerpt truth, but more dangerous than a one-off visible placement issue because it scales quietly.
study_effect: The user hesitates about whether similar issues were treated with the same care.
